Chosen by member state. Systems include:  * [Party list PR](/wiki/Party-list_proportional_representation "Party-list proportional representation") * [STV](/wiki/Single_transferable_vote "Single transferable vote") in Ireland and Malta * *de facto* [FPTP/SMP](/wiki/FPTP "FPTP") (only in the [German-speaking](/wiki/German-speaking_electoral_college "German-speaking electoral college") [electoral college](/wiki/German-speaking_electoral_college "German-speaking electoral college") in Belgium)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Directly elected legislature of the European Union

For the body of the Council of Europe, see [Parliamentary Assembly of the Council of Europe](/wiki/Parliamentary_Assembly_of_the_Council_of_Europe "Parliamentary Assembly of the Council of Europe"). For the last elections, see [2024 European Parliament election](/wiki/2024_European_Parliament_election "2024 European Parliament election").

The **European Parliament** (**EP**) is one of the two [legislative bodies](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature") of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (EU) and one of its [seven institutions](/wiki/Institutions_of_the_European_Union "Institutions of the European Union"). Together with the [Council of the European Union](/wiki/Council_of_the_European_Union "Council of the European Union") (known as the Council and informally as the Council of Ministers), it adopts European legislation, following a proposal by the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"). The Parliament is composed of 720 [members](/wiki/Member_of_the_European_Parliament "Member of the European Parliament") (MEPs), after the June 2024 European elections, from a previous 705 MEPs. It represents the second-largest democratic electorate in the world (after the [Parliament of India](/wiki/Parliament_of_India "Parliament of India")), with an electorate of around 375 million eligible [voters](/wiki/Voting "Voting") in 2024.[[4]](#cite_note-13)[[5]](#cite_note-14)[[6]](#cite_note-18_new_MEPs_take_their_seats-15)

Since 1979, the Parliament has been directly elected every five years by the [citizens of the European Union](/wiki/Citizenship_of_the_European_Union "Citizenship of the European Union") through [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage"). [Voter turnout](/wiki/Voter_turnout "Voter turnout") in parliamentary elections decreased each time after [1979](/wiki/1979_European_Parliament_election "1979 European Parliament election") until [2019](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election "2019 European Parliament election"), when voter turnout increased by eight percentage points, and rose above 50% for the first time since [1994](/wiki/1994_European_Parliament_election "1994 European Parliament election").[[7]](#cite_note-16) The [voting age](/wiki/Voting_age "Voting age") is 18 in all [EU member states](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union") except for [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria") and [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), where it is 16, and [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), where it is 17.[[8]](#cite_note-17)

The European Parliament has legislative power in that the adoption of EU legislation normally requires its approval, and that of the Council, in what amounts to a bicameral legislature. However, it does not formally possess the [right of initiative](/wiki/Right_of_initiative_(legislative) "Right of initiative (legislative)") (i.e. the right to formally initiate the legislative procedure) in the way that most [national parliaments](/wiki/National_parliaments_of_the_European_Union "National parliaments of the European Union") of the member states do, as the right of initiative is a prerogative of the European Commission.[[9]](#cite_note-Parliament's_powers-18)[[10]](#cite_note-Pot_initiative-19) Nonetheless, the Parliament and the Council each have the right to request the Commission to initiate the legislative procedure and put forward a proposal.[[11]](#cite_note-20)

The Parliament is, in protocol terms, the "first institution" of the European Union (mentioned first in [its treaties](/wiki/Treaties_of_the_European_Union "Treaties of the European Union") and having ceremonial precedence over the other EU institutions),[[12]](#cite_note-Protocol-21) and shares equal legislative and [budgetary powers](/wiki/Budget_of_the_European_Union "Budget of the European Union") with the Council (except on a few issues where [special legislative procedures](/wiki/Legislature_of_the_European_Union#Special_legislative_procedures "Legislature of the European Union") apply). It likewise has equal control over the [EU budget](/wiki/Budget_of_the_European_Union "Budget of the European Union"). Ultimately, the European Commission, which serves as the executive branch of the EU, is accountable to Parliament. In particular, Parliament can decide whether or not to approve the European Council's nominee for [President of the Commission](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Commission "President of the European Commission"), and is further tasked with approving (or rejecting) the appointment of the Commission as a whole. It can subsequently force the current Commission to resign by adopting a [motion of censure](/wiki/Motion_of_censure "Motion of censure").[[9]](#cite_note-Parliament's_powers-18)

The [president of the European Parliament](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Parliament "President of the European Parliament") is the body's [speaker](/wiki/Speaker_(politics) "Speaker (politics)") and presides over the multi-party chamber. The five largest political groups are the [European People's Party Group](/wiki/European_People%27s_Party_Group "European People's Party Group") (EPP), the [Progressive Alliance of Socialists and Democrats](/wiki/Progressive_Alliance_of_Socialists_and_Democrats "Progressive Alliance of Socialists and Democrats") (S&D), [Patriots for Europe](/wiki/Patriots_for_Europe "Patriots for Europe") (PfE), the [European Conservatives and Reformists Group](/wiki/European_Conservatives_and_Reformists_Group "European Conservatives and Reformists Group") (ECR), and [Renew Europe](/wiki/Renew_Europe "Renew Europe") (Renew). The last EU-wide election was [held in 2024](/wiki/2024_European_Parliament_election "2024 European Parliament election").

The Parliament's headquarters are officially in [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg"), France,[[13]](#cite_note-treaty-22) and has [its administrative offices](/wiki/Secretariat_of_the_European_Parliament "Secretariat of the European Parliament") in [Luxembourg City](/wiki/Luxembourg_City "Luxembourg City"). [Plenary sessions](/wiki/Plenary_session "Plenary session") are normally held in Strasbourg for four days a month, but sometimes there are additional sessions in Brussels,[[14]](#cite_note-about-eu-23) while the Parliament's committee meetings are held primarily in Brussels, Belgium.[[14]](#cite_note-about-eu-23)[[15]](#cite_note-Consolidated_Treaty-24) In practice, the Parliament works three weeks per month in Brussels and one week (four days) in Strasbourg.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [History of the European Union](/wiki/History_of_the_European_Union "History of the European Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Flag_of_the_European_Parliament_%281973-1983%29.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_European_Parliament_%281973-1983%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_European_Parliament_(1973-1983).svg)

The European Parliament's flag until 1983

The Parliament, like the other EU institutions, was not designed in its current form when it first met on 10 September 1952. One of the oldest common institutions, it began as the **Common Assembly** of the [European Coal and Steel Community](/wiki/European_Coal_and_Steel_Community "European Coal and Steel Community") (ECSC). It was a consultative assembly of 78 appointed parliamentarians [drawn](/wiki/Dual_mandate "Dual mandate") from the national parliaments of member states, having no legislative powers.[[16]](#cite_note-ENA_History-25)[[17]](#cite_note-26) The change since its foundation was highlighted by Professor [David Farrell](/wiki/David_Farrell_(political_scientist) "David Farrell (political scientist)") of the [University of Manchester](/wiki/University_of_Manchester "University of Manchester"): "For much of its life, the European Parliament could have been justly labelled a 'multi-lingual talking shop' [but] is now one of the most powerful legislatures in the world"."[[18]](#cite_note-Farrell_Powerful-27) This was done notably through successive treaty changes and other agreements.[[19]](#cite_note-28)

Its development since its foundation shows how the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union")'s structures have evolved without a clear 'master plan'. [Tom Reid](/wiki/T._R._Reid "T. R. Reid") of *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* has said of the union that "nobody would have deliberately designed a government as complex and as redundant as the EU".[[20]](#cite_note-29) Even the Parliament's [three working locations](/wiki/Location_of_European_Union_institutions#European_Parliament "Location of European Union institutions") are a result of various disagreements and partial agreements among the governments of the Member States. Although most [MEPs](/wiki/Member_of_the_European_Parliament "Member of the European Parliament") would prefer to be based just in Brussels, where it conducts the bulk of its work, at the 1992 Edinburgh [summit](/wiki/European_Council "European Council") chaired by [John Major](/wiki/John_Major "John Major"), France engineered an agreement whereby the European Parliament's official seat is in Strasbourg, but most of its work is done in Brussels.[[16]](#cite_note-ENA_History-25)[[21]](#cite_note-30)

### Consultative assembly

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Consultative assembly")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F023908-0002%2C_Stra%C3%9Fburg%2C_Tagung_des_Europarates.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F023908-0002%2C_Stra%C3%9Fburg%2C_Tagung_des_Europarates.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F023908-0002,_Stra%C3%9Fburg,_Tagung_des_Europarates.jpg)

Session of the Parliamentary Assembly of the Council of Europe in the former [House of Europe](/wiki/House_of_Europe "House of Europe") in Strasbourg, France in January 1967. [Willy Brandt](/wiki/Willy_Brandt "Willy Brandt"), [West German minister for Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Minister_for_Foreign_Affairs_(Germany) "Minister for Foreign Affairs (Germany)"), is speaking.

The body was not mentioned in the original [Schuman Declaration](/wiki/Schuman_Declaration "Schuman Declaration"). It was assumed or hoped that difficulties with the British[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] would be resolved to allow the [Parliamentary Assembly of the Council of Europe](/wiki/Parliamentary_Assembly_of_the_Council_of_Europe "Parliamentary Assembly of the Council of Europe") to perform legislative tasks. A separate Assembly was introduced during negotiations on the Treaty as an institution to counterbalance and monitor the [executive](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") while providing democratic legitimacy.[[16]](#cite_note-ENA_History-25) The wording of the [ECSC Treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1951) "Treaty of Paris (1951)") demonstrated leaders' desire for more than a normal consultative assembly by allowing for direct election and using the term "representatives of the people". Its early importance was highlighted when the Assembly was given the task of drawing up the draft treaty to establish a [European Political Community](/wiki/European_Political_Community_(1952) "European Political Community (1952)"). By this document, the Ad Hoc Assembly was established on 13 September 1952[[22]](#cite_note-AEIpolitical_union_draft_treaty_1-31) with extra members, but after the failure of the negotiated and proposed [European Defence Community](/wiki/European_Defence_Community "European Defence Community") (French parliament veto), the project was dropped.[[23]](#cite_note-ENA_composition-32)

Instead, the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community") and [Euratom](/wiki/Euratom "Euratom") were established in 1958 by the [Treaties of Rome](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rome "Treaty of Rome"). The Common Assembly was shared by all three communities (which had separate executives) and it renamed itself the **European Parliamentary Assembly**.[[16]](#cite_note-ENA_History-25) The first meeting was held on 19 March 1958 having been set up in Luxembourg City, it elected Schuman as its president and on 13 May it rearranged itself to sit according to political ideology rather than nationality.[[24]](#cite_note-1958_history-33) This is seen as the birth of the modern European Parliament, with Parliament's 50 years celebrations being held in March 2008 rather than 2002.[[25]](#cite_note-50_years-34)

The three communities [merged](/wiki/Merger_Treaty "Merger Treaty") their remaining organs as the [European Communities](/wiki/European_Communities "European Communities") in 1967, and the body's name was changed to the current "European Parliament" in 1962.[[16]](#cite_note-ENA_History-25) In 1970 the Parliament was granted power over areas of the [Communities' budget](/wiki/Budget_of_the_European_Union "Budget of the European Union"), which were expanded to the whole budget in 1975.[[26]](#cite_note-ENA_Budget-35) Under the Rome Treaties, the Parliament should have become elected. However, the Council was required to agree a uniform voting system beforehand, which it failed to do. The Parliament threatened to take the Council to the [European Court of Justice](/wiki/European_Court_of_Justice "European Court of Justice"); this led to a compromise whereby the Council would agree to elections, but with each Member State using its own electoral system, leaving the issue of a uniform voting systems to be decided at a later date.[[27]](#cite_note-Hoskyns-36)

For its sessions the assembly, and later the parliament, until 1999 convened in the same premises as the [Parliamentary Assembly of the Council of Europe](/wiki/Parliamentary_Assembly_of_the_Council_of_Europe "Parliamentary Assembly of the Council of Europe"): the House of Europe[[28]](#cite_note-37)[[29]](#cite_note-38) until 1977, and the [Palace of Europe](/wiki/Palace_of_Europe "Palace of Europe") until 1999.

### Elected Parliament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Elected Parliament")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Europa_Parlament_1985.jpg/250px-Europa_Parlament_1985.jpg)](/wiki/File:Europa_Parlament_1985.jpg)

A plenary session in the [Palace of Europe](/wiki/Palace_of_Europe "Palace of Europe") in April 1985, in Strasbourg, France. It was the EP's hemicycle until 1999 when a new building was constructed in Strasbourg, France.

In 1979, its members were [directly elected](/wiki/Elections_in_the_European_Union "Elections in the European Union") for the [first time](/wiki/1979_European_Parliament_election "1979 European Parliament election"). This sets it apart from similar institutions such as those of the [Parliamentary Assembly of the Council of Europe](/wiki/Parliamentary_Assembly_of_the_Council_of_Europe "Parliamentary Assembly of the Council of Europe") or [Pan-African Parliament](/wiki/Pan-African_Parliament "Pan-African Parliament") which are appointed.[[16]](#cite_note-ENA_History-25)[[30]](#cite_note-39)[[31]](#cite_note-40) After that first election, the parliament held its first session on 17 July 1979, electing [Simone Veil](/wiki/Simone_Veil "Simone Veil") MEP as its president.[[32]](#cite_note-41) Veil was also the first female president of the Parliament since it was formed as the Common Assembly.

As an elected body, the Parliament began to draft proposals addressing the functioning of the EU. For example, in 1984, inspired by its previous work on the Political Community, it drafted the "draft Treaty establishing the European Union" (also known as the 'Spinelli Plan' after its rapporteur [Altiero Spinelli](/wiki/Altiero_Spinelli "Altiero Spinelli") MEP). Although it was not adopted as such by the Member States, many of its ideas were later taken up in subsequent amending treaties.[[33]](#cite_note-ENA_TEU-42) Furthermore, the Parliament began holding votes on proposed [Commission Presidents](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Commission "President of the European Commission") from the 1980s, before it was given any formal right to veto their appointment.[[34]](#cite_note-Commission_Appointment-43)

Since it became an elected body, the membership of the European Parliament has expanded when new nations have joined (the membership was also adjusted upwards in 1994 after [German reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification")). Following this, the [Treaty of Nice](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nice "Treaty of Nice") imposed a cap on the number of members to be elected: 732, later raised to 751 by the [Treaty of Lisbon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon "Treaty of Lisbon").[[16]](#cite_note-ENA_History-25)

Like the other institutions, the Parliament's [seat](/wiki/Seat_(legal_entity) "Seat (legal entity)") was not yet fixed. The provisional arrangements placed Parliament in [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg"), while the Commission and Council had their seats in Brussels. In 1985 the Parliament, wishing to be closer to these institutions, built a second chamber in Brussels and moved some of its work there despite protests from some states. A final agreement was eventually reached by the [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council") in 1992. It stated the Parliament would retain its formal seat in Strasbourg, where twelve sessions a year would be held, but with all other parliamentary activity in Brussels. This two-seat arrangement was contested by the Parliament, but was later enshrined in the [Treaty of Amsterdam](/wiki/Treaty_of_Amsterdam "Treaty of Amsterdam"). To this day the [institution's locations](/wiki/Location_of_European_Union_institutions "Location of European Union institutions") are a source of contention.[[35]](#cite_note-ENA_seats-44)

The Parliament gained more powers from successive revisions of the EU treaties, notably through the extension of the [ordinary legislative procedure](/wiki/Ordinary_legislative_procedure "Ordinary legislative procedure") (originally called the codecision procedure),[[36]](#cite_note-ENA_legis-45) and the right to approve international agreements through the consent procedure.

In 1999, the Parliament forced the resignation of the [Santer Commission](/wiki/Santer_Commission "Santer Commission").[[37]](#cite_note-46) The Parliament had refused to approve the [Community budget](/wiki/Budget_of_the_European_Union "Budget of the European Union") over allegations of fraud and mis-management in the commission. The two main parties took on a government-opposition dynamic for the first time during the crisis which ended in the Commission resigning en masse, the first of any forced resignation, in the face of an impending censure from the Parliament.[[38]](#cite_note-Ringe-47)

### Commission accountability to the Parliament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Commission accountability to the Parliament")]

The Parliament had always had the right to dismiss the European Commission in a vote of censure, but it initially had no role in its appointment. In the 1992 [Treaty of Maastricht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Maastricht "Treaty of Maastricht"), the Member States gave the Parliament the right to approve or reject an incoming Commission. In the 1997 [Treaty of Amsterdam](/wiki/Treaty_of_Amsterdam "Treaty of Amsterdam"), they gave it the right to approve or reject an incoming [Commission President](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Commission "President of the European Commission"), subsequently (in the [Treaty of Lisbon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon "Treaty of Lisbon")) describing Parliament's vote as the "election" of the President, and requiring the European Council to make its proposal to Parliament in light of the results of the European elections.[[39]](#cite_note-Constitution_info-48)

In 2004, following the largest trans-national election in history, the European Council proposed as Commission President a candidate, [José Manuel Barroso](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Manuel_Barroso "José Manuel Barroso"), from the largest political party (the EPP). The Parliament approved him by 431 votes to 251. However, when it came to the vote on the Commission as a whole, MEPs raised doubts about some of the nominees following their performance in the public hearings of them conducted by Parliament's committees. Most notably, the [Civil Liberties committee](/wiki/Committee_on_Civil_Liberties,_Justice_and_Home_Affairs "Committee on Civil Liberties, Justice and Home Affairs") rejected [Rocco Buttiglione](/wiki/Rocco_Buttiglione "Rocco Buttiglione") for the post of [Commissioner for Justice, Freedom and Security](/wiki/European_Commissioner_for_Justice,_Freedom_and_Security "European Commissioner for Justice, Freedom and Security") over his views on homosexuality. That was the first time the Parliament had ever opposed an incoming Commissioner and, despite Barroso's initial insistence upon Buttiglione, the Parliament forced Buttiglione to be withdrawn. A number of other Commissioners also had to be withdrawn or reassigned before Parliament voted to allow the [Barroso Commission](/wiki/Barroso_Commission "Barroso Commission") to take office.[[40]](#cite_note-IHT_Butt-49)[[41]](#cite_note-50)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Bolkestein.png/250px-Bolkestein.png)](/wiki/File:Bolkestein.png)

Parliament's overhaul of the [Bolkestein directive](/wiki/Bolkestein_directive "Bolkestein directive") signalled a major growth in status for Parliament.

The Parliament also became more assertive in amending legislative proposals put forward by the European Commission. A notable example was on the [Bolkestein directive](/wiki/Bolkestein_directive "Bolkestein directive") in 2006, when the Parliament voted by a large majority for over 400 amendments that changed the fundamental principle of the law. The *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")* described it in the following terms:[[42]](#cite_note-FT_CIO-51)

> That is where the European parliament has suddenly come into its own. It marks another shift in power between the three central EU institutions. Last week's vote suggests that the directly elected MEPs, in spite of their multitude of ideological, national and historical allegiances, have started to coalesce as a serious and effective EU institution, just as enlargement has greatly complicated negotiations inside both the Council and Commission.

— "How the European parliament got serious", *Financial Times* (23 February 2006)

In 2007, for the first time, Justice Commissioner [Franco Frattini](/wiki/Franco_Frattini "Franco Frattini") included Parliament in talks on the second [Schengen Information System](/wiki/Schengen_Information_System "Schengen Information System") even though, in this field at the time, MEPs only needed to be consulted on parts of the package. After that experiment, Frattini indicated he would like to include Parliament in all justice and criminal matters, informally pre-empting the new powers they were due to gain in 2009 as part of the [Treaty of Lisbon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon "Treaty of Lisbon").[[43]](#cite_note-Frattini_2009-52)

### Recent history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Recent history")]

Further information: [Barroso Commission](/wiki/Barroso_Commission "Barroso Commission")

Between 2007 and 2009, a [special working group on parliamentary reform](/wiki/Special_working_group_on_parliamentary_reform "Special working group on parliamentary reform") implemented a series of changes to modernise the institution such as more speaking time for rapporteurs, increased committee co-operation and other efficiency reforms.[[44]](#cite_note-REFO12-53)[[45]](#cite_note-REFO3-54)

The Lisbon Treaty came into force on 1 December 2009, granting Parliament powers over the entire [EU budget](/wiki/Budget_of_the_European_Union "Budget of the European Union"), making Parliament's [legislative powers](/wiki/Ordinary_legislative_procedure "Ordinary legislative procedure") equal to the Council's in nearly all areas and describing Parliament's vote on an incoming [Commission President](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Commission "President of the European Commission") Commission President as an "election", with the European Council having to make its proposal to Parliament in light of the results of the European elections.[[39]](#cite_note-Constitution_info-48)

Barroso gained the support of the European Council for a second term and secured majority support from the Parliament in September 2009. Parliament voted 382 votes in favour and 219 votes against (117 abstentions) with support of the [European People's Party](/wiki/European_People%27s_Party "European People's Party"), [European Conservatives and Reformists Party](/wiki/European_Conservatives_and_Reformists_Party "European Conservatives and Reformists Party") and the [Alliance of Liberals and Democrats for Europe](/wiki/Alliance_of_Liberals_and_Democrats_for_Europe "Alliance of Liberals and Democrats for Europe").[[46]](#cite_note-B2V-55) The liberals gave support after Barroso gave them a number of concessions; the liberals previously joined the socialists' call for a delayed vote (the EPP had wanted to approve Barroso in July of that year).[[47]](#cite_note-B2C-56)

Once Barroso put forward the candidates for his next Commission, another case of MEPs opposing a particular nominee arose. Bulgarian nominee [Rumiana Jeleva](/wiki/Rumiana_Jeleva "Rumiana Jeleva") was forced to step down by Parliament due to concerns over her experience and financial interests. She only had the support of the EPP which began to retaliate on left wing candidates before Jeleva gave in and was replaced (setting back the final vote further).[[48]](#cite_note-RJ-57)

Before the final vote on the Commission, Parliament demanded a number of concessions as part of a future working agreement under the new Lisbon Treaty. The deal includes that Parliament's president will attend high level Commission meetings. Parliament will have an observer seat in the EU's Commission-led international negotiations and have a right to information on agreements. Parliament did not secure an explicit vote over the appointment of [delegation](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_of_the_European_Union "List of diplomatic missions of the European Union") heads and [special representatives](/wiki/European_Union_Special_Representative "European Union Special Representative") for foreign policy, but it was agreed that they will appear before parliament after they have been appointed by the [High Representative](/wiki/High_Representative "High Representative"). Parliament wanted a pledge from the Commission that it would automatically put forward legislation when parliament requests. Barroso considered this an infringement on the Commission's powers but did agree to respond within three months. Most requests are already responded to positively.[[49]](#cite_note-B2A-58)

During the setting up of the [European External Action Service](/wiki/European_External_Action_Service "European External Action Service") (EEAS), Parliament used its control over the EU budget to influence the shape of the EEAS. MEPs had aimed at getting greater oversight over the EEAS by linking it to the Commission and having political deputies to the [High Representative](/wiki/High_Representative_of_the_Union_for_Foreign_Affairs_and_Security_Policy "High Representative of the Union for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy"). MEPs did not manage to get everything they demanded. However, they got broader financial control over the new body.[[50]](#cite_note-EEAS-59)[[51]](#cite_note-60)

In December 2017, [Politico](/wiki/Politico "Politico") denounced the insufficient racial diversity among Members of the European Parliament.[[52]](#cite_note-61) The subsequent news coverage contributed to create the Brussels So White movement to campaign to rectify this situation.[[53]](#cite_note-62)

On gender balance, some 37 percent of MEPs were women in the 2014–19 Parliament and 40 percent in the 2019–24 Parliament, a greater proposition than in most national Parliaments in Member States [[54]](#cite_note-63) In January 2019, MEPs supported proposals to boost opportunities for women and tackle sexual harassment in the European Parliament.[[55]](#cite_note-64)

In 2022, four people were arrested because of corruption. This came to be known as the [Qatar corruption scandal at the European Parliament](/wiki/Qatar_corruption_scandal_at_the_European_Parliament "Qatar corruption scandal at the European Parliament").[[56]](#cite_note-65)

In October 2023, the Parliament adopted a resolution to condemn "Hamas' despicable terrorist attacks against Israel".[[57]](#cite_note-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/MEPs_condemn_Hamas_attack_on_Israel_and_call_for_a_humanitarian_pause_-_53271623613.jpg/250px-MEPs_condemn_Hamas_attack_on_Israel_and_call_for_a_humanitarian_pause_-_53271623613.jpg)](/wiki/File:MEPs_condemn_Hamas_attack_on_Israel_and_call_for_a_humanitarian_pause_-_53271623613.jpg)

Moment of silence for victims of Hamas' terrorist attack on Israel, October 2023

Since then, Europe's initial and almost unconditional support for Israel started to dwindle due to the disproportionate [attacks on the civilian population](/wiki/Israeli_war_crimes_in_the_Gaza_war "Israeli war crimes in the Gaza war") of [Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") and the [Occupied West Bank](/wiki/Occupied_West_Bank "Occupied West Bank"), with tens of thousands of deaths reported. In July 2025, the EU was weighing partly suspending parts of the [EU-Israel Association Agreement](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93European_Union_relations "Israel–European Union relations") over reports the country is breaching its human rights obligations in Gaza.[[58]](#cite_note-67) A majority for a suspension was not found, but the EU’s 27 foreign ministers agreed to "keep a close watch" on Israel’s compliance with a recent agreement to improve humanitarian aid access into Gaza. They also delayed agreeing on a list of 10 options to respond to Israel's action in Gaza during a meeting of the Foreign Affairs Council.[[59]](#cite_note-68)

The Parliament and Council have been compared to the two [chambers](/wiki/Chambers_of_parliament "Chambers of parliament") of a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") legislature.[[60]](#cite_note-69) However, there are some differences from [national legislatures](/wiki/National_parliaments_of_the_European_Union "National parliaments of the European Union"); for example, neither the Parliament nor the Council have the power of [legislative initiative](/wiki/Legislative_initiative "Legislative initiative") (except for the fact that the Council has the power in some [intergovernmental](/wiki/Intergovernmentalism#A_theory_of_regional_integration "Intergovernmentalism") matters). In [Community matters](/wiki/European_Community "European Community"), this is a power uniquely reserved for the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") (the executive). Therefore, while Parliament can amend and reject legislation, to make a proposal for legislation, it needs the Commission to draft a [bill](/wiki/Bill_(proposed_law) "Bill (proposed law)") before anything can become law.[[61]](#cite_note-70) The value of a right of initiative has anyway been questioned by noting that in the national legislatures of the member states 85% of initiatives introduced without executive support fail to become law.[[62]](#cite_note-Kreppel-71) Yet it has been argued by former Parliament president [Hans-Gert Pöttering](/wiki/Hans-Gert_P%C3%B6ttering "Hans-Gert Pöttering") that as the Parliament does have the right to ask the Commission to draft such legislation, and as the Commission is following Parliament's proposals more and more Parliament does have a *de facto* right of legislative initiative.[[10]](#cite_note-Pot_initiative-19)

The Parliament also has a great deal of indirect influence, through [non-binding resolutions](/wiki/European_Union_law#Legislation "European Union law") and [committee hearings](/wiki/Committees_of_the_European_Parliament "Committees of the European Parliament"), as a "pan-European [soapbox](/wiki/Soapbox "Soapbox")" with the ear of [thousands of Brussels-based journalists](/wiki/Brussels_and_the_European_Union#Lobbyists_and_journalists "Brussels and the European Union"). There is also an indirect effect on [foreign policy](/wiki/Common_Foreign_and_Security_Policy "Common Foreign and Security Policy"); the Parliament must approve all development grants, including those overseas. For example, the support for [post-war Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War") reconstruction, or incentives for the cessation of [Iranian nuclear development](/wiki/Nuclear_program_of_Iran "Nuclear program of Iran"), must be supported by the Parliament. Parliamentary support was also required for the [transatlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") passenger data-sharing deal with the United States.[[63]](#cite_note-Rockwell-72) Finally, Parliament holds a non-binding vote on new EU treaties and treaties amending the EU treaties, but cannot veto them. However, when Parliament threatened to vote down the Nice Treaty, the [Belgian](/wiki/Belgian_Federal_Parliament "Belgian Federal Parliament") and [Italian Parliaments](/wiki/Parliament_of_Italy "Parliament of Italy") said that if it did so, they would veto the treaty on the European Parliament's behalf.[[64]](#cite_note-73)

### Legislative procedure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Legislative procedure")]

With each new treaty, the powers of the Parliament, in terms of its role in the [Union's legislative procedures](/wiki/Ordinary_legislative_procedure "Ordinary legislative procedure"), have expanded. The procedure which has slowly become dominant is the "[ordinary legislative procedure](/wiki/Ordinary_legislative_procedure "Ordinary legislative procedure")" (previously named "codecision procedure"), which provides an equal footing between Parliament and Council. In particular, under the procedure, the Commission presents a proposal to Parliament and the Council which can only become law if both agree on a text, which they do (or not) through successive readings up to a maximum of three. In its first reading, Parliament may send amendments to the Council which can either adopt the text with those amendments or send back a "common position". That position may either be approved by Parliament, or it may reject the text by an [absolute majority](/wiki/Majority "Majority"), causing it to fail, or it may adopt further amendments, also by an absolute majority. If the Council does not approve these, then a "[Conciliation Committee](/wiki/Trilogue_meeting "Trilogue meeting")" is formed. The committee is composed of the Council members plus an equal number of MEPs who seek to agree a compromise. Once a position is agreed, it has to be approved by Parliament, by a simple majority.[[9]](#cite_note-Parliament's_powers-18)[[65]](#cite_note-decision_making-74) This is also aided by Parliament's mandate as the only directly democratic institution, which has given it leeway to have greater control over legislation than other institutions, for example over its changes to the [Bolkestein directive](/wiki/Bolkestein_directive "Bolkestein directive") in 2006.[[42]](#cite_note-FT_CIO-51)

In practice, most legislation is adopted at the first reading stage after the Parliament and the Council, having set out their initial positions, then negotiate a compromise text.[[66]](#cite_note-75) These negotiations take place in so-called "trilogue" meetings, in which the Commission is also present.

In a few areas, *special legislative procedures* apply. These include justice and home affairs, budget and taxation, and certain aspects of other policy areas, such as the fiscal aspects of environmental policy. In these areas, the Council or Parliament decide law alone after consulting the other (or with its consent).[[67]](#cite_note-lisbon_explain-76)

There are different types of [European Union law](/wiki/European_Union_law#Legislation "European Union law").[[9]](#cite_note-Parliament's_powers-18) The strongest act is a [regulation](/wiki/Regulation_(European_Union) "Regulation (European Union)"), an [act](/wiki/Act_of_Parliament "Act of Parliament") or [law](/wiki/Statutory_law "Statutory law") which is directly applicable in its entirety. Then there are [directives](/wiki/Directive_(European_Union) "Directive (European Union)") which bind member states to certain goals which they must achieve. They do this through their own laws and hence have room to manoeuvre in deciding upon them. A [decision](/wiki/European_Union_decision "European Union decision") is an instrument which is applicable to a particular person or group. Institutions may also issue [recommendations and opinions](/wiki/European_Union_recommendation "European Union recommendation") which are merely non-binding, declarations.[[68]](#cite_note-77)

### Budget

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Budget")]

The Parliament and the Council are also the Union's budgetary authority since the [Budgetary Treaties](/wiki/Budgetary_treaties_of_the_European_Communities "Budgetary treaties of the European Communities") of the 1970s and the [Lisbon Treaty](/wiki/Lisbon_Treaty "Lisbon Treaty"). The [EU budget](/wiki/Budget_of_the_European_Union "Budget of the European Union") is subject to a form of the ordinary legislative procedure with a single reading giving Parliament power over the entire budget (before 2009, its influence was limited to certain areas) on an equal footing to the Council. If there is a disagreement between them, it is taken to a conciliation committee as it is for legislative proposals. If the joint conciliation text is not approved by the Council, the Parliament may adopt the budget definitively, but only by a three-fifths majority.[[67]](#cite_note-lisbon_explain-76)

The Parliament is also responsible for discharging the implementation of previous budgets based on the annual report of the [European Court of Auditors](/wiki/European_Court_of_Auditors "European Court of Auditors"). It has refused to grant discharge only twice, in 1984 and in 1998. On the latter occasion it led to the resignation of the [Santer Commission](/wiki/Santer_Commission "Santer Commission"); highlighting how the discharge power gives Parliament a great deal of power over the Commission.[[27]](#cite_note-Hoskyns-36)[[38]](#cite_note-Ringe-47)[[69]](#cite_note-budget_background-78) Parliament also makes extensive use of its budgetary, and other powers, elsewhere; for example in the setting up of the [European External Action Service](/wiki/European_External_Action_Service "European External Action Service"), Parliament had a de facto veto over its design as it has to approve the budgetary and staff changes.[[50]](#cite_note-EEAS-59)

### Control of the executive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Control of the executive")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/5a/European_Parliament_in_2025.jpg/250px-European_Parliament_in_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:European_Parliament_in_2025.jpg)

European Parliament in 2025

The [President of the European Commission](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Commission "President of the European Commission") is proposed by the European Council on the basis of the European elections to Parliament.[[39]](#cite_note-Constitution_info-48) That proposal has to be approved by the Parliament (by a majority of members of the Parliament) who thereby "elect" the President according to the treaties. Following the approval of the Commission President, the members of the Commission are proposed by the President in accord with the member states. Each Commissioner comes before a relevant parliamentary committee hearing covering the proposed portfolio. They are then, as a body, approved or rejected by the Parliament.[[70]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-79)[[71]](#cite_note-Commission_Oversight-80)

In practice, the Parliament has never voted against a President or his Commission, but the threat to do so has produced concessions to Parliament on the Commission's composition or on policy commitments. As described above, when the Barroso Commission was put forward, the Parliament forced the proposal to be withdrawn and changed to be more acceptable to Parliament.[[40]](#cite_note-IHT_Butt-49) That pressure was seen as an important sign by some of the evolving nature of the Parliament and its ability to make the Commission accountable, rather than being a rubber stamp for candidates. Furthermore, in voting on the Commission, MEPs also vote along party lines, rather than national lines, despite frequent pressure from national governments on their MEPs. This cohesion and willingness to use the Parliament's power ensured greater attention from national leaders, other institutions and the public – reversing the previous decline in turnout for the Parliament's elections.[[72]](#cite_note-81)

The Parliament also has the power to censure the Commission by a two-thirds majority which will force the resignation of the entire Commission from office. As with approval, this power has never been explicitly used, but when faced with such a vote, the [Santer Commission](/wiki/Santer_Commission "Santer Commission") then [resigned of their own accord](/wiki/Santer_Commission#Resignation "Santer Commission").

There are other control instruments, such as: the requirement of Commission to submit reports to the Parliament and answer written and oral questions from MEPs; the requirement of the President-in-office of the Council to present its programme at the start of their [presidency](/wiki/Presidency_of_the_Council_of_the_European_Union "Presidency of the Council of the European Union"); the obligation on the President of the [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council") to report to Parliament after each of its meetings; the right of MEPs to make requests for legislation and policy to the Commission; and the right to question members of those institutions (e.g. "Commission [Question Time](/wiki/Question_Time "Question Time")" every Tuesday).[[34]](#cite_note-Commission_Appointment-43)[[71]](#cite_note-Commission_Oversight-80) Regarding written and oral questions, MEPs voted in July 2008 to limit questions to those within the EU's mandate and ban offensive or personal questions.[[73]](#cite_note-EUO_rise-82)

### Supervisory and scrutiny powers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Supervisory and scrutiny powers")]

The Parliament also has other powers of general supervision, mainly granted by the [Maastricht Treaty](/wiki/Maastricht_Treaty "Maastricht Treaty").[[74]](#cite_note-83) The Parliament has the power to set up a Committee of Inquiry, which it has done, for example over mad cow disease and CIA detention flights – the former led to the creation of the [European veterinary agency](/wiki/European_Medicines_Agency "European Medicines Agency"). The Parliament can call other institutions to answer questions and if necessary to take them to [court](/wiki/Court_of_Justice_of_the_European_Communities "Court of Justice of the European Communities") if they break EU law or treaties.[[75]](#cite_note-supervisory-84) Furthermore, it has powers over the appointment of the members of the Court of Auditors[[76]](#cite_note-85) and the president and executive board of the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank"). The [ECB president](/wiki/List_of_Presidents_of_the_European_Central_Bank "List of Presidents of the European Central Bank") is also obliged to present an annual report to the parliament.[[75]](#cite_note-supervisory-84)

The [European Ombudsman](/wiki/European_Ombudsman "European Ombudsman") is elected by the Parliament to deal with public complaints about maladministration (administrative irregularities, unfairness, discrimination, abuse of power, failure to reply, refusal of information or unnecessary delay) by any EU institution or body.[[75]](#cite_note-supervisory-84)

Petitions can be brought to the Parliament by any EU citizen on a matter within the EU's sphere of activities. The Parliament's [Committee on Petitions](/wiki/Committee_on_Petitions "Committee on Petitions") hears cases, some 1500 each year, sometimes presented by the citizen themselves at the Parliament. While the Parliament attempts to resolve the issue as a mediator they do resort to legal proceedings if it is necessary to resolve the citizens dispute.[[77]](#cite_note-petitions-86)
