[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/GenderOfMEPs2024.png/250px-GenderOfMEPs2024.png)](/wiki/File:GenderOfMEPs2024.png)

Gender of the members of the European Parliament

The [parliamentarians](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament "Member of Parliament") are known in English as [Members of the European Parliament](/wiki/Members_of_the_European_Parliament "Members of the European Parliament") (MEPs). They are elected every five years by [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") and sit according to political allegiance. About 40 percent are women. Before the [first direct elections, in 1979](/wiki/1979_European_Parliament_election "1979 European Parliament election"), they were appointed by their national parliaments.[[23]](#cite_note-ENA_composition-32)[[78]](#cite_note-EP_Members-87)

The Parliament has been criticized for underrepresentation of minority groups. In 2017, an estimated 17 MEPs were non-white,[[79]](#cite_note-88) and of these, three were black, a disproportionately low number.[[80]](#cite_note-89) According to activist organization [European Network Against Racism](/wiki/European_Network_Against_Racism "European Network Against Racism"), while an estimated 10% of Europe is composed of racial and ethnic minorities, only 5% of MEPs were members of such groups following the [2019 European Parliament election](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election "2019 European Parliament election").[[81]](#cite_note-Psaledakis2020-90)

Under the [Lisbon Treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon "Treaty of Lisbon"), [seats are allocated](/wiki/Apportionment_in_the_European_Parliament "Apportionment in the European Parliament") to each state according to population and the maximum number of members is set at 751.[[82]](#cite_note-EUO_Lisbon-91) Since 1 February 2020 and the United Kingdom's [leaving the EU](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit"), 705 MEPs sit in the European Parliament. It rose to 720 after the 2024 elections.

Representation is currently limited to a maximum of 96 seats and a minimum of 6 seats per state and the seats are distributed according to "[degressive proportionality](/wiki/Degressive_proportionality "Degressive proportionality")", i.e., the larger the state, the more citizens are represented per MEP. As a result, Maltese and Luxembourgish voters have roughly 10x more influence per voter than citizens of the six largest countries.

As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit), Germany (80.9 million inhabitants) has 96 seats (previously 99 seats), i.e. one seat for 843,000 inhabitants. Malta (0.4 million inhabitants) has 6 seats, i.e. one seat for 70,000 inhabitants.

The new system implemented under the Lisbon Treaty, including revising the seating well before elections, was intended to avoid political [horse trading](/wiki/Horse_trading_(political) "Horse trading (political)") when the allocations have to be revised to reflect demographic changes.[[83]](#cite_note-92)

Pursuant to this apportionment, the [constituencies](/wiki/European_Parliament_constituency "European Parliament constituency") are formed. In four EU member states (Belgium, Ireland, Italy and Poland), the national territory is divided into a number of constituencies. In the remaining member states, the whole country forms a single constituency. All member states hold elections to the European Parliament using various forms of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation").

### Transitional arrangements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Transitional arrangements")]

Due to the delay in ratifying the Lisbon Treaty, the [seventh parliament](/wiki/7th_European_Parliament "7th European Parliament") was elected under the lower Nice Treaty cap. A [small scale treaty amendment](/wiki/Treaties_of_the_European_Union#Transitional_provisions_protocol "Treaties of the European Union") was ratified on 29 November 2011.[[84]](#cite_note-93) This amendment brought in transitional provisions to allow the 18 additional MEPs created under the Lisbon Treaty to be elected or appointed before the 2014 election.[[85]](#cite_note-94) Under the Lisbon Treaty reforms, Germany was the only state to lose members from 99 to 96. However, these seats were not removed until the 2014 election.[[86]](#cite_note-extra_meps-95)

### Salaries and expenses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Salaries and expenses")]

Before 2009, members received the same salary as members of their national parliament. However, from 2009 a new members statute came into force, after years of attempts, which gave all members an equal monthly pay, of €8,484.05 each in 2016, subject to a European Union tax and which can also be taxed nationally. MEPs are entitled to a pension, paid by Parliament, from the age of 63. Members are also entitled to allowances for office costs and subsistence, and travelling expenses, based on actual cost.[[87]](#cite_note-96) Besides their pay, members are granted a number of privileges and [immunities](/wiki/Diplomatic_immunity "Diplomatic immunity"). To ensure their free movement to and from the Parliament, they are accorded by their own states the facilities accorded to senior officials travelling abroad and, by other state governments, the status of visiting [foreign representatives](/wiki/Diplomat "Diplomat"). When in their own state, they have all the immunities accorded to national parliamentarians, and, in other states, they have immunity from [detention](/wiki/Detention_(imprisonment) "Detention (imprisonment)") and [legal proceedings](/wiki/Criminal_procedure "Criminal procedure"). They may then only be prosecuted after the European Parliament waives their immunity. However, immunity cannot be claimed when a member is found committing a criminal offence and the Parliament also has the right to strip a member of their immunity.[[88]](#cite_note-LEX_Prot-97)

### Political groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Political groups")]

Main article: [Political groups of the European Parliament](/wiki/Political_groups_of_the_European_Parliament "Political groups of the European Parliament")

MEPs in Parliament are organised into eight different [parliamentary groups](/wiki/Parliamentary_group "Parliamentary group"); members who do not join a group are known as *non-attached members* or *[non-inscrits](/wiki/Non-inscrits "Non-inscrits")*.

The two largest groups are the [European People's Party Group](/wiki/European_People%27s_Party_Group "European People's Party Group") (EPP) and the [Socialists & Democrats](/wiki/Progressive_Alliance_of_Socialists_and_Democrats "Progressive Alliance of Socialists and Democrats") (S&D). These two groups have dominated the Parliament for much of its existence, jointly holding between 50 and 70 percent of the seats until 2019. No single group has ever held an absolute majority in Parliament.[[89]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-98)

As a result of being broad alliances of national parties, political groups in the European Parliament are decentralised and have more in common with parties in federal states like Germany or the United States than with unitary states, like the majority of the EU member states.[[62]](#cite_note-Kreppel-71) Nevertheless, the political groups were actually more cohesive than their US counterparts between 2004 and 2009.[[90]](#cite_note-99)[[91]](#cite_note-100)

Half of the groups are based on a single [European political party](/wiki/European_political_party "European political party"), such as the [European People's Party](/wiki/European_People%27s_Party "European People's Party"), with the addition of MEPs from national parties not members of a European party and independent MEPs. The others, such as the [Greens–European Free Alliance](/wiki/Greens%E2%80%93European_Free_Alliance "Greens–European Free Alliance") group or the [European Conservatives and Reformists Group](/wiki/European_Conservatives_and_Reformists_Group "European Conservatives and Reformists Group"), center on two European parties, as well as other national parties and independents.[[92]](#cite_note-101)

Groups need 23 MEPs from seven different member states to register. Following registration, groups receive public funding from the budget of the European Parliament.[[93]](#cite_note-102)

### Coalitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Coalitions")]

Given that the Parliament does not form the government in the traditional sense of many parliamentary systems, majorities are built issue by issue. Generally, this is along broadly [consensual](/wiki/Consensus_democracy "Consensus democracy") lines with dynamic coalitions[[94]](#cite_note-103) rather than [majority rule](/wiki/Majority_rule "Majority rule") of competing parties and coalitions.

These coalitions usually involve the [European People's Party](/wiki/European_People%27s_Party "European People's Party") and the Socialist and Democrat Group of the [Party of European Socialists](/wiki/Party_of_European_Socialists "Party of European Socialists") who tend to co-operate to find compromises between them and then bring in others. Their relationship has been described as a [grand coalition](/wiki/Grand_coalition "Grand coalition").[[95]](#cite_note-104) However, this does not always produce agreement, and each may instead try to build other alliances, the EPP normally with other centre-right or right wing Groups and the PES with centre-left or left wing groups. Sometimes, the Liberal (Renew Europe) Group is then in the pivotal position.

There are also occasions where very sharp party political divisions have emerged, for example over the [resignation of the Santer Commission](/wiki/Santer_Commission#Budget_controversy "Santer Commission").[[38]](#cite_note-Ringe-47) When the initial allegations against the Commission emerged, they were directed primarily against [Édith Cresson](/wiki/%C3%89dith_Cresson "Édith Cresson") and [Manuel Marín](/wiki/Manuel_Mar%C3%ADn "Manuel Marín"), both socialist members. When the parliament was considering refusing to discharge the [Community budget](/wiki/Budget_of_the_European_Union "Budget of the European Union"), President [Jacques Santer](/wiki/Jacques_Santer "Jacques Santer") stated that a no vote would be tantamount to a [vote of no confidence](/wiki/Vote_of_no_confidence "Vote of no confidence"). The Socialist Group initially supported the Commission and saw the issue as an attempt by the EPP to discredit their party ahead of the 1999 elections. Socialist leader, [Pauline Green](/wiki/Pauline_Green "Pauline Green") MEP, attempted a vote of confidence and the EPP put forward counter motions. During this period the two parties took on similar roles to a government-[opposition](/wiki/Opposition_(parliamentary) "Opposition (parliamentary)") dynamic, with the Socialists supporting the executive and EPP renouncing its previous coalition support and voting it down.[[38]](#cite_note-Ringe-47) Politicisation such as this has been increasing, in 2007 Simon Hix of the [London School of Economics](/wiki/London_School_of_Economics "London School of Economics") noted that:[[18]](#cite_note-Farrell_Powerful-27)

> Our work also shows that politics in the European Parliament is becoming increasingly based around party and ideology. Voting is increasingly split along left-right lines, and the cohesion of the party groups has risen dramatically, particularly in the fourth and fifth parliaments. So there are likely to be policy implications here too.

During the fifth term, 1999 to 2004, there was a break in the grand coalition resulting in a centre-right coalition between the Liberal and People's parties.[[96]](#cite_note-Watson_interview-105) This was reflected in the Presidency of the Parliament with the terms being shared between the EPP and the Liberals (then called ELDR), rather than the EPP and Socialists.[[97]](#cite_note-106) In the following term the liberal group grew to hold 88 seats, the largest number of seats held by any third party in Parliament.[[98]](#cite_note-ALDE_formed-107) The EPP-S&D coalition lost their majority after the [2019 European Parliament election](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election "2019 European Parliament election"),[[99]](#cite_note-108) requiring support by at least one other political group for a majority, most frequently the liberal Renew Group or the Greens.

## Elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Elections")]

Main article: [Elections to the European Parliament](/wiki/Elections_to_the_European_Parliament "Elections to the European Parliament")

Elections have taken place, directly in every member state, every five years since 1979. As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit) there have been ten elections. When a nation joins mid-term, a by-election is held in that country to elect its representatives. This has happened six times, most recently when Croatia joined in 2013. Elections take place on over a four-day period from a Thursday to a Sunday (each Member State votes on the day of its choice).

Member States choose their electoral system within common guidelines, notably that the suffrage must be universal and that seats are allocated by [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation"). This includes determining constituencies: while most Member States have a national constituencies, some divide their allocation between regions. Seats are [allocated to member states](/wiki/Apportionment_in_the_European_Parliament "Apportionment in the European Parliament") according to their population, since 2014 with no state having more than 96, but no fewer than 6, to maintain proportionality.[[100]](#cite_note-109)

The most recent Union-wide elections to the European Parliament were the [European elections of 2019](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election "2019 European Parliament election"), held from 23 to 26 May 2019. They were the largest simultaneous transnational elections ever held anywhere in the world.
The first session of the ninth parliament started 2 July 2019.[[101]](#cite_note-110)

[European political parties](/wiki/European_political_parties "European political parties") have the exclusive right to campaign during the European elections (as opposed to their corresponding EP groups).[[102]](#cite_note-111) There have been a number of proposals designed to attract greater public attention to the elections. One such innovation in the 2014 elections was that the [pan-European political parties](/wiki/European_political_party "European political party") announced, ahead of the elections, their candidates for [president](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Commission "President of the European Commission") of the [Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"), the so-called *Spitzenkandidaten* (German, "leading candidates" or "top candidates"). The President of the European Commission is nominated by the European Council, representing the governments of the member states, and while there is no obligation for them to nominate the successful "candidate", the Lisbon Treaty states that they should take account of the results of the elections when choosing whom to nominate, and the candidate they propose must be endorsed by a majority of members of the European Parliament.[[103]](#cite_note-112)

The *Spitzenkandidaten* in 2014 were [Jean-Claude Juncker](/wiki/Jean-Claude_Juncker "Jean-Claude Juncker") for the [European People's Party](/wiki/European_People%27s_Party "European People's Party"),[[104]](#cite_note-113) [Martin Schulz](/wiki/Martin_Schulz "Martin Schulz") for the [Party of European Socialists](/wiki/Party_of_European_Socialists "Party of European Socialists"), [Guy Verhofstadt](/wiki/Guy_Verhofstadt "Guy Verhofstadt") for the [Alliance of Liberals and Democrats for Europe Party](/wiki/Alliance_of_Liberals_and_Democrats_for_Europe_Party "Alliance of Liberals and Democrats for Europe Party"), [Ska Keller](/wiki/Ska_Keller "Ska Keller") and [José Bové](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Bov%C3%A9 "José Bové") jointly for the [European Green Party](/wiki/European_Green_Party "European Green Party") and [Alexis Tsipras](/wiki/Alexis_Tsipras "Alexis Tsipras") for the [Party of the European Left](/wiki/Party_of_the_European_Left "Party of the European Left"). The EPP won the most seats and Jean-Claude Juncker was nominated by the European Council and elected by the Parliament.

In 2019, the parties again announced their candidates for President of the Commission ahead of the European elections, notably [Manfred Weber](/wiki/Manfred_Weber "Manfred Weber") for the EPP and [Frans Timmermans](/wiki/Frans_Timmermans "Frans Timmermans") for the Party of European Socialists. However, after the election, there was no agreement among the parties after the election to back any of them. After a period of deadlock, the European Council proposed [Ursula von der Leyen](/wiki/Ursula_von_der_Leyen "Ursula von der Leyen") as a compromise and Parliament elected her, albeit by a narrow majority.

In 2024, the EPP decided to back Von der Leyen for a second term of office and put her forward as their "Spitzenkandidat" while the PES put forward [Nicolas Schmit](/wiki/Nicolas_Schmit "Nicolas Schmit"). After the election, the European Council duly nominated her and the Parliament elected her by 401 votes to 284, with support from the EPP, the Socialists, the liberal Renew Group and the Greens.[[105]](#cite_note-114)

Until 2014, turnout dropped consistently in every election since the first election, and from 1999 until 2014 was below 50%. In 2007 both [Bulgaria and Romania elected their MEPs](/wiki/2007_European_Parliament_election "2007 European Parliament election") in by-elections, having joined at the beginning of 2007. The Bulgarian and Romanian elections saw two of the lowest turnouts for European elections, just 28.6%[[106]](#cite_note-115) and 28.3% respectively.[[107]](#cite_note-116) This trend was interrupted in the 2019 election, when turnout increased by 8% EU-wide, rising to 50.6%, the highest since 1994.

In England, Scotland and Wales, EP elections were originally held for a constituency MEP on a first-past-the-post basis. In 1999 the system was changed to a form of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") where parties put up a team of candidates in regional constituencies.[[108]](#cite_note-117) One could vote for a party's list of candidates, but not for an individual candidate (unless that party had a single candidate).

## Proceedings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Proceedings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/CantinesEUbrussel.jpg/250px-CantinesEUbrussel.jpg)](/wiki/File:CantinesEUbrussel.jpg)

These "relocation boxes" of the European Parliament, called "cantines", are ready to be transported between [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg") and [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"). The EP moves back and forth to meet the EU treaty obligation to hold plenary sessions in Strasbourg.

Formally, the European Parliament has an annual "session", divided into monthly "part-sessions" and daily "sittings". In practice, this entails a monthly cycle with two committee weeks where matters are discussed in committees, political group weeks for members to discuss work within their political groups, and session weeks where members spend 3+1⁄2 days in [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg") for part-sessions. In addition six 2-day part sessions are organised in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") throughout the year. Four weeks a year are allocated as constituency week to allow members to do exclusively constituency work, which they otherwise have to do at weekends. Finally there are no meetings planned during the summer weeks.[[109]](#cite_note-118)

The Parliament has the power to meet without being convened by another authority. Its meetings are partly set out by the treaties but are otherwise up to Parliament according to its own "Rules of Procedure" (the regulations governing the parliament).[[110]](#cite_note-rules-119)

During sittings, members may speak after being called on by the President. Members of the Council or Commission may also attend and speak in debates.[[111]](#cite_note-120)[[112]](#cite_note-121) Partly due to the need for interpretation, and the politics of consensus in the chamber, debates tend to be calmer and more polite than, say, the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system").[[113]](#cite_note-122) Voting is conducted primarily by a show of hands, that may be checked on request by electronic voting.[[114]](#cite_note-123) Votes of MEPs are not recorded in either case, however; that only occurs when there is a roll-call ballot. This is required for the final votes on legislation and also whenever a political group or 30 MEPs request it. The number of roll-call votes has increased with time. Votes can also be a completely secret ballot (for example, when the president is elected).[[115]](#cite_note-124)[[116]](#cite_note-125) All recorded votes, along with minutes and legislation, are recorded in the *[Official Journal of the European Union](/wiki/Official_Journal_of_the_European_Union "Official Journal of the European Union")* and can be accessed online.[[117]](#cite_note-126) Votes usually do not follow a debate, but rather they are grouped with other due votes on specific occasions, usually at noon on Tuesdays, Wednesdays or Thursdays. This is because the length of the vote is unpredictable and if it continues for longer than allocated it can disrupt other debates and meetings later in the day.[[118]](#cite_note-Corbett_174-127)

Members sit in a [hemicycle](/wiki/Hemicycle_(chamber) "Hemicycle (chamber)") according to their political groups (in the Common Assembly, prior to 1958, members sat alphabetically[[119]](#cite_note-Corbett_167-128)) who are ordered mainly by left to right. All desks are equipped with microphones, headphones for listening to interpretation, and electronic voting equipment. The leaders of the Groups sit on the front benches, and in the very centre is a podium for guest speakers. The remaining half of the circular chamber is primarily composed of the raised area where the President and staff sit. Further benches are provided between the sides of this area and the MEPs, these are taken up by the Council on the far left and the Commission on the far right. Both the Brussels and Strasbourg hemicycle roughly follow this layout with only minor differences.[[120]](#cite_note-129) The hemicycle design is a compromise between the different Parliamentary systems. The British-based system has the different groups directly facing each other while the French-based system is a semicircle (and the traditional German system had all members in rows facing a rostrum for speeches). Although the design is mainly based on a semicircle, the opposite ends of the spectrum do still face each other.[[119]](#cite_note-Corbett_167-128)

Access to the chamber limited, entrance is controlled by ushers who also aid MEPs in the chamber (for example in delivering documents). The ushers can also occasionally act as a form of police in enforcing the President, for example in ejecting an MEP who is disrupting the session (although this is rare). The first head of protocol in the Parliament was French, so many of the duties in the Parliament are based on the French model first developed following the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"). The ushers are highly visible in the Parliament, dressed in black [tails](/wiki/Tailcoat "Tailcoat") and wearing a silver chain, and are [recruited](/wiki/EU_Concours "EU Concours") in the same manner as the [European civil service](/wiki/European_Civil_Service "European Civil Service"). The President is allocated a personal usher.[[121]](#cite_note-130)

### President and organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: President and organisation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Roberta_Metsola_elected_new_President_of_the_European_Parliament_%2851828380418%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Roberta_Metsola_elected_new_President_of_the_European_Parliament_%2851828380418%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roberta_Metsola_elected_new_President_of_the_European_Parliament_(51828380418)_(cropped).jpg)

[Roberta Metsola](/wiki/Roberta_Metsola "Roberta Metsola"), the current [President of the European Parliament](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Parliament "President of the European Parliament")

Main article: [President of the European Parliament](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Parliament "President of the European Parliament")

The President is essentially the [speaker](/wiki/Speaker_(politics) "Speaker (politics)") of the Parliament and presides over the plenary when it is in session. The President's signature is required for all acts adopted by co-decision, including the EU budget. The President is also responsible for representing the Parliament externally, including in legal matters, and for the application of the rules of procedure. The President is elected for two-and-a-half-year terms, meaning two elections per parliamentary term.[[122]](#cite_note-131)[[123]](#cite_note-132) The current President of the European Parliament is [Roberta Metsola](/wiki/Roberta_Metsola "Roberta Metsola"), who was elected in January 2022.[[124]](#cite_note-133)

In most countries, the protocol of the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") comes before all others; however, in the EU the Parliament is listed as the first institution, and hence the protocol ranking of its president comes before any other European, or national, protocol. The gifts given to numerous visiting dignitaries depend upon the President. President [Josep Borrell](/wiki/Josep_Borrell "Josep Borrell") MEP of Spain gave his counterparts a crystal cup created by an artist from Barcelona who had engraved upon it parts of the [Charter of Fundamental Rights](/wiki/Charter_of_Fundamental_Rights_of_the_European_Union "Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union") among other things.[[12]](#cite_note-Protocol-21)

A number of notable figures have been President of the Parliament and its predecessors. The first President was [Paul-Henri Spaak](/wiki/Paul-Henri_Spaak "Paul-Henri Spaak") MEP,[[125]](#cite_note-134) one of the [founding fathers of the Union](/wiki/Founding_fathers_of_the_European_Union "Founding fathers of the European Union"). Other founding fathers include [Alcide de Gasperi](/wiki/Alcide_de_Gasperi "Alcide de Gasperi") MEP and [Robert Schuman](/wiki/Robert_Schuman "Robert Schuman") MEP. The three female Presidents were [Simone Veil](/wiki/Simone_Veil "Simone Veil") MEP in 1979 (first President of the elected Parliament), [Nicole Fontaine](/wiki/Nicole_Fontaine "Nicole Fontaine") MEP in 1999, both Frenchwomen, and [Roberta Metsola](/wiki/Roberta_Metsola "Roberta Metsola") from Malta in 2022.[[126]](#cite_note-135) The previous president, [Jerzy Buzek](/wiki/Jerzy_Buzek "Jerzy Buzek") was the first [East-Central European](/wiki/East-Central_Europe "East-Central Europe") to lead an EU institution, a former [Prime Minister of Poland](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Poland "Prime Minister of Poland") who rose out of the [Solidarity](/wiki/Solidarity_(Polish_trade_union) "Solidarity (Polish trade union)") movement in Poland that helped overthrow communism in the [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc").[[127]](#cite_note-136)

During the election of a President, the previous President (or, if unable to, one of the previous vice-presidents) presides over the chamber.[[128]](#cite_note-LeP-137) Prior to 2009, the [oldest member](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Parliament#Oldest_member "President of the European Parliament") fulfilled this role[[129]](#cite_note-138) but the rule was changed to prevent far-right French MEP [Jean-Marie Le Pen](/wiki/Jean-Marie_Le_Pen "Jean-Marie Le Pen") taking the chair.[[128]](#cite_note-LeP-137)

The Parliament also elects 14 [Vice-Presidents](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_European_Parliament "Vice President of the European Parliament") who chair debates when the President is not in the chamber. There are a number of bodies responsible for the running of Parliament. The two main ones are the [Bureau](/wiki/Bureau_(European_Parliament) "Bureau (European Parliament)"), which is responsible for budgetary and administration issues and is composed of the President and the Vice-Presidents, and the [Conference of Presidents](/wiki/Conference_of_Presidents "Conference of Presidents") which is the political governing body and is composed of the President of the Parliament and the leaders of each of the Parliament's political groups. Looking after the financial and administrative interests of members are five [Quaestors](/wiki/Quaestor_(European_Parliament) "Quaestor (European Parliament)").

As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit), the European Parliament budget was EUR 1.756 billion.[[130]](#cite_note-139) A 2008 report on the Parliament's finances highlighted certain overspending and miss-payments. Despite some MEPs calling for the report to be published, Parliamentary authorities had refused until an MEP broke confidentiality and leaked it.[[131]](#cite_note-140)

### Committees and delegations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Committees and delegations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Committee_Room_of_the_European_Parliament_in_Brussels.jpg/250px-Committee_Room_of_the_European_Parliament_in_Brussels.jpg)](/wiki/File:Committee_Room_of_the_European_Parliament_in_Brussels.jpg)

A Committee room

Main article: [Committees of the European Parliament](/wiki/Committees_of_the_European_Parliament "Committees of the European Parliament")

The Parliament has 20 [Standing Committees](/wiki/Standing_Committee "Standing Committee") ranging in size from 25 to 88 MEPs (each reflecting the political make-up of the whole Parliament) including a chair, a bureau and secretariat. They meet twice a month in public to draw up, amend to adopt legislative proposals and reports to be presented to the plenary.[[132]](#cite_note-Organised-141) The [rapporteurs](/wiki/Rapporteur "Rapporteur") for a committee are supposed to present the view of the committee, although notably this has not always been the case: in the events leading to the resignation of the Santer Commission, the rapporteur went against the [Budgetary Control Committee's](/wiki/Committee_on_Budgetary_Control "Committee on Budgetary Control") narrow vote to discharge the budget, and urged the Parliament to reject it.[[38]](#cite_note-Ringe-47)

The Parliament can also set up sub-committees (e.g. the [Subcommittee on Human Rights](/wiki/Subcommittee_on_Human_Rights "Subcommittee on Human Rights")) and temporary committees to deal with a specific topic (e.g. the Special Committee on Foreign Interference in Democratic Processes in the European Union, 2020–23). The chairs of the Committees co-ordinate their work through the "[Conference of Committee Chairmen](/wiki/Committees_of_the_European_Parliament#Conference_of_Committee_Chairs "Committees of the European Parliament")".[[132]](#cite_note-Organised-141)

The nature of the committees differ from their national counterparts as, although smaller in comparison to those of the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congressional_committee "United States Congressional committee"), the European Parliament's committee secretariats are unusually large by European standards with between eight and twelve dedicated members of staff and three to four support staff. Considerable administration, archives and research resources are also at the disposal of the whole Parliament when needed.[[62]](#cite_note-Kreppel-71)

[Delegations of the Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament_Delegations "European Parliament Delegations") are formed in a similar manner and are responsible for relations with Parliaments outside the EU. There are 44 delegations, mostly small (around 15 MEPs) but some larger. Chairpersons of the delegations also cooperate in a conference like the committee chairs do. Delegations include "Interparliamentary delegations" (maintain relations with Parliament outside the EU), "joint parliamentary committees" (maintaining relations with parliaments of states which are candidates or associates of the EU), the delegation to the [ACP EU Joint Parliamentary Assembly](/wiki/ACP_EU_Joint_Parliamentary_Assembly "ACP EU Joint Parliamentary Assembly") and the delegation to the [Euro-Mediterranean Parliamentary Assembly](/wiki/Euro-Mediterranean_Parliamentary_Assembly "Euro-Mediterranean Parliamentary Assembly").[[132]](#cite_note-Organised-141) MEPs also participate in other international activities such as the [Euro-Latin American Parliamentary Assembly](/wiki/Euro-Latin_American_Parliamentary_Assembly "Euro-Latin American Parliamentary Assembly"), the [NATO Assembly](/wiki/NATO_Assembly "NATO Assembly"), the [Transatlantic Legislators' Dialogue](/wiki/Transatlantic_Legislators%27_Dialogue "Transatlantic Legislators' Dialogue") and through [election observation](/wiki/Election_monitoring "Election monitoring") in third countries.[[133]](#cite_note-142)

### Intergroups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Intergroups")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Dvorana_Jo%C5%BEeta_Pu%C4%8Dnika_Jo%C5%BEe_Pu%C4%8Dnik_Hall.jpg/250px-Dvorana_Jo%C5%BEeta_Pu%C4%8Dnika_Jo%C5%BEe_Pu%C4%8Dnik_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dvorana_Jo%C5%BEeta_Pu%C4%8Dnika_Jo%C5%BEe_Pu%C4%8Dnik_Hall.jpg)

[Jože Pučnik](/wiki/Jo%C5%BEe_Pu%C4%8Dnik "Jože Pučnik") conference room

The [Intergroups in the European Parliament](/wiki/Intergroups_in_the_European_Parliament "Intergroups in the European Parliament") are informal fora which gather MEPs from various political groups around any topic. They do not express the view of the European Parliament. They serve a double purpose: to address a topic which is transversal to several committees and in a less formal manner. Their daily secretariat can be run either through the office of MEPs or through interest groups, be them corporate lobbies or NGOs. The favored access to MEPs which the organization running the secretariat enjoys can be one explanation to the multiplication of Intergroups in the 1990s.[[134]](#cite_note-143) They are now strictly regulated and financial support, direct or otherwise (via Secretariat staff, for example), must be officially specified in a declaration of financial interests.[[135]](#cite_note-144)[[136]](#cite_note-145) Also Intergroups are established or renewed at the beginning of each legislature through a specific process. Indeed, the proposal for the constitution or renewal of an Intergroup must be supported by at least 3 political groups who can each support a limited number of proposals in proportion to their size (for example, for the legislature 2014–2019, the EPP or S&D political groups could support 22 proposals whereas the Greens/EFA or the EFDD political groups only 7).[[137]](#cite_note-146)

### Translation and interpretation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Translation and interpretation")]

Interpretation in the European Parliament

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/2007_07_16_parlament_europejski_bruksela_47.JPG/250px-2007_07_16_parlament_europejski_bruksela_47.JPG)](/wiki/File:2007_07_16_parlament_europejski_bruksela_47.JPG)

Interpreting booths in the hemicycle simultaneously interpret debates between 24 languages.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Hearing_of_Commissioner-designate_Thierry_Breton_%2849068586441%29.jpg/250px-Hearing_of_Commissioner-designate_Thierry_Breton_%2849068586441%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hearing_of_Commissioner-designate_Thierry_Breton_(49068586441).jpg)

Interpreter at a committee hearing for the confirmation of [Thierry Breton](/wiki/Thierry_Breton "Thierry Breton") to the European Commission in 2019

See also: [Languages of the European Union](/wiki/Languages_of_the_European_Union "Languages of the European Union")

Speakers in the European Parliament are entitled to speak in any of the 24 official [languages of the European Union](/wiki/Languages_of_the_European_Union "Languages of the European Union"), ranging from French and German to [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese_language "Maltese language") and [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"). Simultaneous interpreting is offered in all plenary sessions, and all final texts of legislation are translated. With twenty-four languages, the European Parliament is the most multilingual parliament in the world[[138]](#cite_note-147) and the biggest employer of interpreters in the world (employing 350 full-time and 400 freelancers when there is higher demand).[[139]](#cite_note-Interpreters-148) Citizens may also address the Parliament in [Basque](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language"), [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language")/[Valencian](/wiki/Valencian_language "Valencian language") and [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language").[[140]](#cite_note-149)

Usually a language is translated from a foreign tongue into a translator's native tongue. Due to the large number of languages, some being minor ones, since 1995 interpreting is sometimes done the opposite way, out of an interpreter's native tongue (the "retour" system). In addition, a speech in a minor language may be interpreted through a third language for lack of interpreters ("relay" interpreting) – for example, when interpreting out of [Estonian](/wiki/Estonian_language "Estonian language") into Maltese.[[139]](#cite_note-Interpreters-148) Due to the complexity of the issues, interpretation is not word for word. Instead, interpreters have to convey the political meaning of a speech, regardless of their own views. This requires detailed understanding of the politics and terms of the Parliament, involving a great deal of preparation beforehand (e.g. reading the documents in question). Difficulty can often arise when MEPs use profanities, jokes and word play or speak too fast.[[139]](#cite_note-Interpreters-148)

While some see speaking their native language as an important part of their identity, and can speak more fluently in debates, interpretation and its cost has been criticised by some. A 2006 report by [Alexander Stubb](/wiki/Alexander_Stubb "Alexander Stubb") MEP highlighted that by only using English, French and German costs could be reduced from [€](/wiki/Euro "Euro")118,000 per day (for 21 languages then – [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"), [Bulgarian](/wiki/Bulgarian_language "Bulgarian language") and [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language") having not yet been included) to €8,900 per day.[[141]](#cite_note-150) There has also been a small-scale campaign to make French the reference language for all legal texts, on the basis of an argument that it is more clear and precise for legal purposes.[[142]](#cite_note-151)

Because the proceedings are translated into all of the official EU languages, they have been used to make a multilingual [corpus](/wiki/Text_corpus "Text corpus") known as [Europarl](/wiki/Europarl_corpus "Europarl corpus"). It is widely used to train [statistical machine translation](/wiki/Statistical_machine_translation "Statistical machine translation") systems.[[143]](#cite_note-152)

## Corruption scandals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Corruption scandals")]

### Qatar related

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Qatar related")]

Main article: [Qatar corruption scandal at the European Parliament](/wiki/Qatar_corruption_scandal_at_the_European_Parliament "Qatar corruption scandal at the European Parliament")

Qatargate is an ongoing political scandal, involving allegations that European Parliament officials, lobbyists and their families have been influenced by the governments of Qatar, Morocco and Mauritania, engaging in corruption, money laundering, and organized crime to improve the image of these countries in the EU[[144]](#cite_note-153) amongst others to kill off six parliamentary resolutions condemning Qatar's human rights record, and working to deliver a visa-free travel deal between Doha and the EU. Law enforcement authorities in Belgium, Italy and Greece seized €1.5 million in cash, confiscated computers and mobile phones, and charged four individuals with the alleged offences:[[145]](#cite_note-154)[[146]](#cite_note-155)[[147]](#cite_note-156)[[148]](#cite_note-157) Eva Kaili, Antonio Panzeri, an Italian former MEP, Francesco Giorgi and Luca Visentini. Later also Niccolò Figà-Talamanca, Marc Tarabella and Andrea Cozzolino were arrested. In 2023, Belgian MEP Marie Arena was charged, and in 2025, two more women: Elisabetta Gualmini and Alessandra Moretti.

### Azerbaijan related

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Azerbaijan related")]

[Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan") has been harshly criticized for bribing foreign officials and diplomats to promote its causes abroad and legitimize its elections at home, a practice termed [caviar diplomacy](/wiki/Caviar_diplomacy "Caviar diplomacy").[[149]](#cite_note-158)[[150]](#cite_note-159)[[151]](#cite_note-160) In 2023 the Swedish-German investigative platform Blankspot published several investigative reports about freewheeling trips made by several European Parliament members to Azerbaijan.[[152]](#cite_note-161) Several publications have emphasised how these trips raise questions on the reliability of EU Parliament ethics codes and the importance of enforcing them.[[153]](#cite_note-162)[[154]](#cite_note-163)

### French National Front assistants affair

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: French National Front assistants affair")]

In December 2023, [Marine Le Pen](/wiki/Marine_Le_Pen "Marine Le Pen") was ordered to stand trial after she was charged with paying National Front party officials through funds earmarked for European Parliament assistants.[[155]](#cite_note-164) Twenty-seven others, including her father Jean Marie, served as her co-defendants.[[156]](#cite_note-165) Her trial, for misappropriation of public funds, was scheduled in March 2024 to occur between 30 September and 27 November the same year.[[157]](#cite_note-166)

On 31 March 2025, eight MEPs, 12 assistants from the National Rally, and Le Pen were found guilty of embezzling European Union funds by a Paris court.[[158]](#cite_note-167) Le Pen received a four-year prison sentence, two years of which were suspended, in addition to a €100,000 fine. She did not begin serving the two years' house arrest immediately as all appeals must be exhausted before this part of the sentence is executed. Similarly, she did not lose her seat in the lower house of the French parliament immediately. On the other hand, the court also banned her, effective immediately, from standing for political office for five years, making her ineligible to run in the 2027 French presidential election.[[159]](#cite_note-168)

### Russia related

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Russia related")]

The former British MEP [Nathan Gill](/wiki/Nathan_Gill "Nathan Gill") ([UKIP](/wiki/UKIP "UKIP") and later the Leader of the [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK") party in Wales) pleaded guilty on Friday, 26 September 2025 to eight counts of bribery, under the UK's Bribery Act, 2010. He had taken payments to speak in the European Parliament to promote Russian interests, in particular in relation to Ukraine.[[160]](#cite_note-169)

### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Other")]

A January 2024 report published by investigative outlet *Follow the Money* found that 1 in 4 EU lawmakers have been implicated in judicial cases or scandals. Out of 253 cases highlighted in the report, only 23 have resulted in convictions.[[161]](#cite_note-170)

There are also ongoing concerns related to allegations that members of the European Parliament were illegally or unethically influenced by Russia. Such concerns have been raised several times in 2023 and 2024 and dubbed "Russiagate".[[162]](#cite_note-171)[[163]](#cite_note-172)[[164]](#cite_note-173)

Panzeri's lobbying group, Fight Impunity, was largely funded by [Iqbal Survé](/wiki/Iqbal_Surv%C3%A9 "Iqbal Survé"), a controversial South African billionaire. Survé's [Sekunjalo Development Foundation](/wiki/Sekunjalo_Investments "Sekunjalo Investments") was found to be the "most important donor" having donated €250,000 to the group.[[165]](#cite_note-174)[[166]](#cite_note-175)

### Anti-corruption policies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Anti-corruption policies")]

The European Parliament has had criticism over its prodigality and for being too complacent with conflicts of interest.[[167]](#cite_note-176)[[168]](#cite_note-:0-177) Its refusal to become full member of the [GRECO](/wiki/Group_of_States_against_Corruption "Group of States against Corruption") like all its member states is also a matter of criticism.[[168]](#cite_note-:0-177)

The European Parliament revised its rules of procedure and its code of conduct in September 2023,[[169]](#cite_note-178) placing six main obligations on MEPs:[[170]](#cite_note-179)[[171]](#cite_note-180)

* To make a detailed Declaration of private interests, including those from the 3 years prior to their election
* When total external income exceeds €5,000, all the entities from which their income is received to be disclosed
* With regard to conflicts of interest, to resolved or declare them
* Not to engage in paid lobbying activities linked to the EU's decision-making process
* To meet only with interested parties who sign up to the EU's Transparency Register and to make disclosure of such meetings, and also of meetings held with representatives of third country diplomats
* To make a Declaration of all assets and liabilities at the beginning, and again at the end, of every term of office.

## Annual costs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Annual costs")]

According to the European Parliament website, the annual parliament budget for 2021 was €2.064 billion, which corresponds to 1.2% of [EU budget](/wiki/Budget_of_the_European_Union "Budget of the European Union").[[172]](#cite_note-181) The main cost categories were:

* 45% – staff (staff expenses, contract agents, linguistic services)
* 22% – operational costs (buildings, IT, administration)
* 26% – political activities (members, activities of political groups, parties and foundations)
* 6% – communications

According to a European Parliament study prepared in 2013, the Strasbourg seat costs an extra €103 million over maintaining a single location and according to the Court of Auditors an additional €5 million is related to travel expenses caused by having two seats.[[173]](#cite_note-182)

As a comparison, the German lower house of parliament ([Bundestag](/wiki/Bundestag "Bundestag")) is estimated to cost €517 million in total for 2018, for a parliament with 709 members.[[176]](#cite_note-185) The [British House of Commons](/wiki/British_House_of_Commons "British House of Commons") reported total annual costs in 2016–2017 of £249 million (€279 million). It had 650 seats.[[177]](#cite_note-186)

According to *The Economist*, the European Parliament costs more than the British, French and German parliaments combined. A quarter of the costs is estimated to be related to translation and interpretation costs (c. €460 million) and the double seats are estimated to add an additional €180 million a year.[[178]](#cite_note-187) For a like-for-like comparison, these two cost blocks can be excluded.

On 2 July 2018, MEPs rejected proposals to tighten the rules around the General Expenditure Allowance (GEA),[[179]](#cite_note-188) which "is a controversial €4,416 per month payment that MEPs are given to cover office and other expenses, but they are not required to provide any evidence of how the money is spent".[[180]](#cite_note-189)

## Seats

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Seats")]

Seats of the European Parliament

[![Espace Léopold building in Brussels, Belgium](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/European_Parliament_%28DSC01793%29.jpg/250px-European_Parliament_%28DSC01793%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:European_Parliament_(DSC01793).jpg)

[Espace Léopold](/wiki/Espace_L%C3%A9opold "Espace Léopold") building in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Flag_of_Belgium_%28civil%29.svg/40px-Flag_of_Belgium_%28civil%29.svg.png) [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium")

[![Konrad Adenauer building in Luxembourg City, Luxembourg](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Luxembourg%2C_Europaparlament_Parlement_europ%C3%A9en_tram_stop_%28104%29.jpg/250px-Luxembourg%2C_Europaparlament_Parlement_europ%C3%A9en_tram_stop_%28104%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Luxembourg,_Europaparlament_Parlement_europ%C3%A9en_tram_stop_(104).jpg)

[Konrad Adenauer](/wiki/Konrad_Adenauer "Konrad Adenauer") building in [Luxembourg City](/wiki/Luxembourg_City "Luxembourg City"), ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Flag_of_Luxembourg.svg/40px-Flag_of_Luxembourg.svg.png) [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg") (seat of the Secretariat-General)

Further information: [Location of European Union institutions](/wiki/Location_of_European_Union_institutions "Location of European Union institutions"), [Espace Léopold](/wiki/Espace_L%C3%A9opold "Espace Léopold"), and [Seat of the European Parliament in Strasbourg](/wiki/Seat_of_the_European_Parliament_in_Strasbourg "Seat of the European Parliament in Strasbourg")

The Parliament is based in three different cities with numerous buildings. The Member States attached a protocol to the [Treaty of Amsterdam](/wiki/Treaty_of_Amsterdam "Treaty of Amsterdam") that requires 12 plenary sessions to be held in [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg") (in practice, none in August but two in October), which is described as the Parliament's official seat, while extra part sessions as well as committee meetings are held in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"). Luxembourg City hosts the [Secretariat of the European Parliament](/wiki/Secretariat_of_the_European_Parliament "Secretariat of the European Parliament").[[15]](#cite_note-Consolidated_Treaty-24) The European Parliament is one of at least two assemblies in the world with more than one meeting place (another being the [parliament of the Isle of Man, Tynwald](/wiki/Tynwald "Tynwald")) and one of the few that does not have the power to decide its own location.[[181]](#cite_note-Strange-190)

The Strasbourg seat is considered a symbol of reconciliation between France and Germany, two countries that previously fought over the region. However, the cost and inconvenience of having two seats is questioned. While Strasbourg is the official seat, and sits alongside the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe"),[[182]](#cite_note-191) [Brussels is home to nearly all other major EU institutions](/wiki/Brussels_and_the_European_Union "Brussels and the European Union"), with the majority of Parliament's work being carried out there. Critics have described the two-seat arrangement as a "travelling circus",[[183]](#cite_note-Seat_Green-192) and there is a strong movement to establish Brussels as the sole seat. This is because the other political institutions (the Commission, Council and European Council) are located there, and hence Brussels is treated as the '[capital](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city")' of the EU. This movement has received strong backing from numerous figures, including [Margot Wallström](/wiki/Margot_Wallstr%C3%B6m "Margot Wallström"), Commission First-Vice President from 2004 to 2010, who stated that "something that was once a very positive symbol of the EU reuniting France and Germany has now become a negative symbol – of wasting money, bureaucracy and the insanity of the Brussels institutions".[[184]](#cite_note-193) The [Green Party](/wiki/European_Green_Party "European Green Party") has also noted the environmental cost in a study led by [Jean Lambert](/wiki/Jean_Lambert "Jean Lambert") MEP and [Caroline Lucas](/wiki/Caroline_Lucas "Caroline Lucas") MEP; in addition to the extra 200 million euro spent on the extra seat, there are over 20,268 tonnes of additional carbon dioxide, undermining any environmental stance of the institution and the Union.[[183]](#cite_note-Seat_Green-192) The campaign is further backed by a million-strong online petition started by [Cecilia Malmström](/wiki/Cecilia_Malmstr%C3%B6m "Cecilia Malmström") MEP.[[185]](#cite_note-194) In August 2014, an assessment by the European Court of Auditors calculated that relocating the Strasbourg seat of the European Parliament to Brussels would save €113.8 million per year.[[186]](#cite_note-195) In 2006, there were allegations of irregularities in the charges made by the city of Strasbourg on buildings the Parliament rented, thus further harming the case for the Strasbourg seat.[[187]](#cite_note-196)

Most MEPs prefer Brussels as a single base.[[188]](#cite_note-197) A poll of MEPs found 89% of the respondents wanting a single seat, and 81% preferring Brussels.[[189]](#cite_note-198) Another survey found 68% support.[[18]](#cite_note-Farrell_Powerful-27) In July 2011, an absolute majority of MEPs voted in favour of a single seat.[[190]](#cite_note-199) In early 2011, the Parliament voted to scrap one of the Strasbourg sessions by holding two within a single week.[[191]](#cite_note-200) The [mayor of Strasbourg](/wiki/Roland_Ries "Roland Ries") officially reacted by stating "we will counter-attack by upturning the adversary's strength to our own profit, as a [judoka](/wiki/Judo "Judo") would do".[[192]](#cite_note-201) However, as Parliament's seat is fixed by the treaties, it can only be changed by the Member States, acting unanimously and with national ratification, meaning that France could veto any move.[[181]](#cite_note-Strange-190) Former French President [Nicolas Sarkozy](/wiki/Nicolas_Sarkozy "Nicolas Sarkozy") stated that the Strasbourg seat is "non-negotiable" and that France has no intention of surrendering the only EU Institution on French soil.[[193]](#cite_note-202) Given France's declared intention to veto any relocation to Brussels, some MEPs have advocated [civil disobedience](/wiki/Civil_disobedience "Civil disobedience") by refusing to take part in the monthly exodus to Strasbourg.[[194]](#cite_note-203)[[195]](#cite_note-204)[[196]](#cite_note-205)

However, the main building in Brussels has been suffering for more than a decade from a state of degradation. Renovation or reconstruction works including a hemicycle were estimated to cost at least €500 million in 2017 with fear that the cost would be even higher[[197]](#cite_note-206) and possibly escalate up to €1 billion,[[198]](#cite_note-207) whereas the seat in Strasbourg already offers a fully-fledged hemicycle.

## Channels of dialogue, information, and communication with European civil society

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Channels of dialogue, information, and communication with European civil society")]

Individual MEPs are the main point of contact for citizens with the Parliament. They normally have an office in their constituency and travel back at the end of every week (and during weeks without parliamentary meetings) to participate in an array of meetings, events, interviews, visits and suchlike with voters, businesses, trade unions, local authorities and media.

The Parliament itself maintains a detailed website,[[199]](#cite_note-208) receives around one million visitors a year, and streamlines debates and committee meetings.

Over the last few years, European institutions have committed to promoting transparency, openness, and the availability of information about their work.[[200]](#cite_note-209) In particular, transparency is regarded as pivotal to the action of European institutions and a general principle of EU law, to be applied to the activities of EU institutions in order to strengthen the Union's democratic foundation.[[201]](#cite_note-210) The general principles of openness and transparency are reaffirmed in the articles 8 A, point 3 and 10.3 of the [Treaty of Lisbon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lisbon "Treaty of Lisbon") and the [Maastricht Treaty](/wiki/Maastricht_Treaty "Maastricht Treaty") respectively, stating that "every citizen shall have the right to participate in the democratic life of the Union. Decisions shall be taken as openly and as closely as possible to the citizen".[[202]](#cite_note-eur-lex.europa.eu-211)[[203]](#cite_note-eur-lex.europa.eu-2-212) Furthermore, both treaties acknowledge the value of dialogue between citizens, representative associations, civil society, and European institutions.[[202]](#cite_note-eur-lex.europa.eu-211)[[203]](#cite_note-eur-lex.europa.eu-2-212)

### Dialogue with religious and non-confessional organisations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Dialogue with religious and non-confessional organisations")]

Article 17 of the [Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Functioning_of_the_European_Union "Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union") (TFEU) lays the juridical foundation for an open, transparent dialogue between European institutions and churches, religious associations, and non-confessional and philosophical organisations.[[204]](#cite_note-europarl.europa.eu-213) In July 2014, in the beginning of the 8th term, then President of the European Parliament [Martin Schulz](/wiki/Martin_Schulz "Martin Schulz") tasked [Antonio Tajani](/wiki/Antonio_Tajani "Antonio Tajani"), then vice-president, with implementing the dialogue with the religious and confessional organisations included in article 17.[[205]](#cite_note-214) In this framework, the European Parliament hosts high-level conferences on inter-religious dialogue, also with focus on current issues and in relation with parliamentary works.[[204]](#cite_note-europarl.europa.eu-213)

### European Parliament Mediator for International Parental Child Abduction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: European Parliament Mediator for International Parental Child Abduction")]

The chair of European Parliament Mediator for International Parental Child Abduction was established in 1987 by initiative of British MEP Charles [Henry Plumb](/wiki/Henry_Plumb "Henry Plumb"), with the goal of helping minor children of international couples victim of parental abduction. The Mediator finds negotiated solutions in the higher interest of the minor when said minor is abducted by a parent following separation of the couple, regardless whether married or unmarried.[[206]](#cite_note-215)[[207]](#cite_note-europarl.europa.eu-2-216) Since its institution, the chair has been held by [Mairead McGuinness](/wiki/Mairead_McGuinness "Mairead McGuinness") (since 2014), Roberta Angelilli (2009–2014), Evelyne Gebhardt (2004–2009), Mary Banotti (1995–2004), and Marie-Claude Vayssade (1987–1994).[[207]](#cite_note-europarl.europa.eu-2-216) The Mediator's main task is to assist parents in finding a solution in the minor's best interest through mediation, i.e. a form of controversy resolution alternative to lawsuit. The Mediator is activated by request of a citizen and, after evaluating the request, starts a mediation process aimed at reaching an agreement. Once subscribed by both parties and the Mediator, the agreement is official. The nature of the agreement is that of a private contract between parties.[[207]](#cite_note-europarl.europa.eu-2-216) In defining the agreement, the European Parliament offers the parties the juridical support necessary to reach a sound, lawful agreement based on legality and equity. The agreement can be ratified by the competent national courts and can also lay the foundation for consensual separation or divorce.[[207]](#cite_note-europarl.europa.eu-2-216)

### Liaison Offices (EPLOs) & Antennas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Liaison Offices (EPLOs) & Antennas")]

The European Parliament operates Liaison Offices (EPLOs) in all Member States, as well as in the United Kingdom ([London](/wiki/London "London") and the United States ([Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")).[[208]](#cite_note-217) On 17 September 2025, European Parliament President [Roberta Metsola](/wiki/Roberta_Metsola "Roberta Metsola") opened the Parliament's antenna office in Kyiv, co-located with the [EU delegation](/wiki/Delegation_of_the_European_Union_to_Ukraine "Delegation of the European Union to Ukraine") in [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv").[[209]](#cite_note-218) The European Parliament also operates antennas in [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") (for the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations")), [Addis Ababa](/wiki/Addis_Ababa "Addis Ababa") (for the [African Union](/wiki/African_Union "African Union")) [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") (for [ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN")), [Tirana](/wiki/Tirana "Tirana") (for the Western [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans")), [Chișinău](/wiki/Chi%C8%99in%C4%83u "Chișinău") (for [Eastern Partnership](/wiki/Eastern_Partnership "Eastern Partnership")). All antennas are co-located within the [EEAS](/wiki/EEAS "EEAS") delegations.

## European Parliamentary Research Service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: European Parliamentary Research Service")]

The [European Parliamentary Research Service](/wiki/European_Parliamentary_Research_Service "European Parliamentary Research Service") (EPRS) is the European Parliament's in-house research department and think tank. It provides [Members of the European Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_the_European_Parliament "Member of the European Parliament") – and, where appropriate, [parliamentary committees](/wiki/Committees_of_the_European_Parliament "Committees of the European Parliament") – with independent, objective and authoritative analysis of, and research on, policy issues relating to the European Union, in order to assist them in their parliamentary work. It is also designed to increase Members' and EP committees' capacity to scrutinise and oversee the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") and other [EU executive bodies](/wiki/Bodies_of_the_European_Union "Bodies of the European Union").

EPRS aims to provide a comprehensive range of products and services, backed by specialist internal expertise and knowledge sources in all policy fields, so empowering Members and committees through knowledge and contributing to the Parliament's effectiveness and influence as an institution. In undertaking this work, the EPRS supports and promotes parliamentary outreach to the wider public, including dialogue with relevant stakeholders in the [EU](/wiki/European_Union "European Union")'s system of multi-level governance. All EPRS publications are publicly available on the EP Think Tank platform.[[210]](#cite_note-219)[[211]](#cite_note-220)

## Eurobarometer of the European Parliament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Eurobarometer of the European Parliament")]

The European Parliament periodically commissions opinion polls and studies on public opinion trends in member states to survey perceptions and expectations of citizens about its work and the overall activities of the European Union. Topics include citizens' perception of the European Parliament's role, their knowledge of the institution, their sense of belonging in the European Union, opinions on European elections and European integration, identity, citizenship, political values, but also on current issues such as climate change, current economy and politics, etc. [Eurobarometer](/wiki/Eurobarometer "Eurobarometer") analyses seek to provide an overall picture of national situations, regional specificities, socio-demographic cleavages, and historical trends.[[212]](#cite_note-221)[[213]](#cite_note-222)

## Prizes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Prizes")]

### Sakharov Prize

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Sakharov Prize")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Remise_du_Prix_Sakharov_%C3%A0_Aung_San_Suu_Kyi_Strasbourg_22_octobre_2013-11.jpg/250px-Remise_du_Prix_Sakharov_%C3%A0_Aung_San_Suu_Kyi_Strasbourg_22_octobre_2013-11.jpg)](/wiki/File:Remise_du_Prix_Sakharov_%C3%A0_Aung_San_Suu_Kyi_Strasbourg_22_octobre_2013-11.jpg)

The ceremony of the [Sakharov Prize](/wiki/Sakharov_Prize "Sakharov Prize") awarded to [Aung San Suu Kyi](/wiki/Aung_San_Suu_Kyi "Aung San Suu Kyi") by [Martin Schulz](/wiki/Martin_Schulz "Martin Schulz"), in 2013

With the [Sakharov Prize](/wiki/Sakharov_Prize "Sakharov Prize"), created in 1988, the European Parliament supports human rights by awarding individuals that contribute to promoting human rights worldwide, thus raising awareness on human rights violations. Priorities include: protection of human rights and fundamental liberties, with particular focus on freedom of expression; protection of minority rights; compliance with international law; and development of democracy and authentic rule of law.[[214]](#cite_note-europarl.europa.eu-3-223)[[215]](#cite_note-224)

### European Charlemagne Youth Prize

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: European Charlemagne Youth Prize")]

The [European Charlemagne Youth Prize](/wiki/European_Charlemagne_Youth_Prize "European Charlemagne Youth Prize") seeks to encourage youth participation in the European integration process. It is awarded by the European Parliament and the Foundation of the International Charlemagne Prize of Aachen to youth projects aimed at nurturing common European identity and European citizenship.[[214]](#cite_note-europarl.europa.eu-3-223)

### European Citizens' Prize

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: European Citizens' Prize")]

The [European Citizens' Prize](/wiki/European_Citizens%27_Prize "European Citizens' Prize") is awarded by the European Parliament to activities and actions carried out by citizens and associations to promote integration between the citizens of EU member states and transnational cooperation projects in the EU.[[214]](#cite_note-europarl.europa.eu-3-223)

### LUX Prize

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: LUX Prize")]

Since 2007, the [LUX Prize](/wiki/LUX_Prize "LUX Prize") is awarded by the European Parliament to films dealing with current topics of public European interest that encourage reflection on Europe and its future. Over time, the Lux Prize has become a prestigious cinema award which supports European film and production also outside the EU.[[216]](#cite_note-225)

### Daphne Caruana Galizia Journalism Prize

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=European_Parliament&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Daphne Caruana Galizia Journalism Prize")]

From 2021, the Daphne Caruana Galizia Journalism prize shall be awarded by the European Parliament to outstanding journalism that reflect EU values. The prize consists in an award of 20,000 euros. This award is named after the late Maltese journalist, [Daphne Caruana Galizia](/wiki/Daphne_Caruana_Galizia "Daphne Caruana Galizia") who was assassinated in Malta on 16 October 2017.[[217]](#cite_note-226) In October 2021 the prize was first awarded to the [Pegasus Project](/wiki/Pegasus_Project_(investigation) "Pegasus Project (investigation)").[[218]](#cite_note-227)
